philosophy: Use as little AI as possible — combine deterministic logic with LLMs.
Ship small, iterate fast. Proof of concept before polish.
Every layer should work independently before integrating with the next.
Tie pricing and value to measurable outcomes, not feature counts.
If you turn the system off and nobody calls within an hour, it's not solving a real problem.

patterns: service-layer: business logic lives in services, not routes
repository-pattern: database access abstracted behind repository interfaces
dependency-injection: FastAPI Depends() for composable dependencies
separation-of-concerns: routes handle HTTP, services handle logic, models handle data
error-hierarchy: custom exception classes with typed error responses
async-by-default: async endpoints, sync only when forced by a blocking library
principles: Routes are thin — validate input, call service, return response.
Never import provider SDKs outside the service layer.
Background tasks go through Celery, not FastAPI BackgroundTasks (except fire-and-forget logging).
Every endpoint has input validation (Pydantic), output schema, and error responses documented.
Migrations are always reversible — every upgrade has a matching downgrade.
Environment variables for all configuration — never hardcode secrets, URLs, or feature flags.
key_libraries: fastapi: API framework with automatic OpenAPI docs
pydantic: data validation and settings management
celery: distributed task queue for background processing
redis: message broker for Celery + caching layer
httpx: async HTTP client for external API calls
tenacity: retry logic for external service calls

patterns: single-database: PostgreSQL handles relational data AND vectors (pgvector) — no separate vector DB needed to start
connection-pooling: use connection pool for all database access
migration-driven: all schema changes go through Alembic, never manual SQL
soft-delete: prefer soft deletes (is_deleted flag) over hard deletes for audit trail
indexed-queries: every query that filters or joins should have a supporting index
principles: PostgreSQL is enough — Instagram scaled to hundreds of millions on just Postgres.
Don't add a separate vector database until pgvector becomes a proven bottleneck.
Redis is for caching and message brokering, not as a primary data store.
Every table has created_at, updated_at timestamps.
Foreign keys are enforced at the database level, not just the application level.
Use UUIDs for public-facing IDs, auto-increment integers for internal references.
scaling_path: Start: single PostgreSQL instance with pgvector
Growth: add read replicas, connection pooling (PgBouncer)
Scale: partition large tables, consider dedicated vector DB (Weaviate/Chroma) only when pgvector query time exceeds SLA

patterns: component-composition: small, reusable components over monolithic pages
shadcn-imports: components are imported into the project (not node_modules) so AI agents can modify them
colocation: keep component, styles, types, and tests together
optimistic-ui: update UI immediately, reconcile with server response
loading-states: every async operation has loading, success, and error states
principles: shadcn/ui for all standard components — don't reinvent buttons, modals, tables, forms.
TypeScript strict mode — no any types except at external API boundaries.
Tailwind for styling — no CSS modules, no styled-components, no inline styles.
Every page works on mobile — responsive by default using Tailwind breakpoints.
API calls go through a centralized client with auth headers, error interceptors, and retry logic.
No direct DOM manipulation — everything through React state and refs.
key_libraries: vite: dev server and build tool
shadcn-ui: importable component library (not a dependency — lives in your codebase)
zod: schema validation (shared patterns with Pydantic on backend)

patterns: minimal-ai: use deterministic logic first, LLMs only when reasoning or generation is required
structured-outputs: always request structured (JSON) outputs from LLMs, validate with Pydantic
chain-of-responsibility: break complex tasks into sequential steps, each independently testable
rag-pipeline: ingest → chunk → embed → store → retrieve → generate → validate
cognitive-architecture: design data flow diagrams before writing AI code
guardrails: prompt injection detection, PII filtering, output validation on every LLM call
fallback-chains: if primary model fails, fall back to secondary, then to deterministic response
principles: The AI layer is a thin wrapper — most of the system is regular software engineering.
Every LLM call is traced (input, output, latency, token cost) via Langfuse.
Never expose raw LLM output to users without validation and sanitization.
Embeddings are generated once at ingestion time, not at query time (except for the query itself).
Use hybrid search (semantic + keyword) for retrieval — pure semantic search misses exact matches.
LLM-as-a-judge for automated evaluation, but always validate against human-annotated datasets.
Provider SDKs are isolated behind a service interface — switching providers should be a config change.
Context windows are finite — chunk documents intelligently, don't stuff everything in.

techniques: contextual-retrieval: add document context to chunks before embedding
query-expansion: rephrase user query for broader recall
re-ranking: score and re-order retrieved chunks by relevance
self-query: extract metadata filters from natural language queries

evaluation: retrieval-precision: are the retrieved chunks relevant?
retrieval-recall: did we miss relevant chunks?
answer-faithfulness: is the answer grounded in the retrieved context?
answer-relevance: does the answer address the user's question?

key_libraries: langchain: LLM orchestration, chains, document loaders, text splitters
langgraph: stateful agent workflows with cycles and branching
langfuse: LLM observability, tracing, prompt management
tiktoken: token counting for context window management

patterns: container-per-service: backend, frontend, worker, redis, postgres each in their own container
docker-compose-environments: separate compose files for dev, test, and production
health-checks: every service exposes a /health endpoint
twelve-factor: config in environment, logs to stdout, stateless processes
blue-green-deployment: zero-downtime deploys by running new version alongside old
load-balancing: Nginx as reverse proxy with upstream blocks for horizontal scaling
principles: Docker Compose for development and single-server production — Kubernetes only at true scale.
Every service has a health check that verifies its dependencies (DB connection, Redis ping).
Secrets are never in Docker images or git — use environment variables injected at runtime.
HTTPS everywhere — Certbot/Let's Encrypt for automated certificate management.
Logs are structured JSON to stdout — centralized logging is a layer on top, not baked in.
CI/CD runs tests, builds images, and deploys on merge to main — no manual deploys.
Monitor cost, latency, and error rates post-deployment — not just 'is it up?'

patterns: cache-aside: check cache first, fall back to DB, populate cache on miss
ttl-based: all cache entries have explicit TTL, no indefinite caching
cache-invalidation: invalidate on write, not on timer

load_balancing.scaling_path: vertical first (bigger VPS), horizontal when CPU consistently >70%

patterns: idempotent-tasks: every task can be safely retried
dead-letter-queue: failed tasks after N retries go to DLQ for manual inspection
priority-queues: separate queues for urgent (auth, notifications) vs. bulk (ingestion, reports)
task-timeout: every task has a max execution time

use_cases: user-created: triggers welcome email and default settings

scaling_path: Start: single VPS with Docker Compose (backend + frontend + postgres + redis)
Growth: separate database to managed PostgreSQL, add Celery workers
Scale: load balancer (Nginx) + multiple backend instances, CDN for frontend
Enterprise: container orchestration (ECS/Kubernetes), managed Redis, read replicas

patterns: defense-in-depth: multiple layers of security, not a single perimeter
least-privilege: every role gets minimum required permissions
fail-secure: on error, deny access rather than grant it
input-validation: validate all user input at the API boundary (Pydantic)
output-encoding: sanitize all output to prevent XSS
principles: Never store plaintext passwords — bcrypt with sufficient work factor.
JWT access tokens are short-lived (15-30 min), refresh tokens are long-lived and rotated.
CORS is restrictive by default — whitelist specific origins, not '*'.
Rate limit authentication endpoints aggressively (10/min per IP).
All secrets in environment variables, never in code or config files committed to git.
SQL injection is prevented by parameterized queries (SQLAlchemy handles this) — never raw string concatenation.
File uploads are validated (type, size, content) and stored outside the web root.
API keys are hashed in the database, not stored in plaintext.
HTTPS enforced everywhere — no HTTP endpoints in production.
Audit log for all authentication events and permission changes.
ai_security: Prompt injection detection on all user inputs that reach LLM calls.
PII filtering on LLM outputs before returning to users.
Output validation — LLM responses are validated against expected schema before use.
Context isolation — user A's documents are never retrievable by user B.
Token budget enforcement — no single request can consume unbounded tokens.
Model output is never trusted as code execution input without sanitization.

track: request-latency: p50, p95, p99 per endpoint
llm-cost: tokens consumed and dollar cost per call
llm-latency: time to first token, total generation time
retrieval-quality: precision and recall per RAG query
task-queue-depth: Celery queue length and processing time

principles: Every LLM call is traced end-to-end: input, output, latency, tokens, cost.
Structured logs (JSON) with correlation IDs across request lifecycle.
Alerts on error rate spikes, not just individual errors.
Health checks verify actual functionality (can query DB, can reach Redis), not just process uptime.

patterns: unit-tests: service layer logic, utility functions, validators
integration-tests: API endpoints with real database (not mocks)
llm-tests: deterministic inputs with expected output patterns, LLM-as-judge for quality
rag-evaluation: retrieval precision/recall on annotated dataset
e2e-tests: critical user flows through the full stack
principles: Integration tests hit a real database — mocks hide real bugs.
LLM outputs are tested for structure and constraints, not exact text.
Every bug fix comes with a regression test.
Tests run in Docker to match production environment.
CI blocks merge on test failure — no exceptions.

claude: all sections — reads stack profile to validate agent recommendations
